cards.q: What are the four architectural constraint dimensions and their mitigations?
cards.a: Latency → parallelization & caching. Accuracy → structured intermediates & few-shot. Cost → Batch APIs & context pruning. Compliance → application-layer intercepts (NOT prompts).

Relying on emphatic system prompts ('CRITICAL POLICY: NEVER process >$500') still yields ~3% failure. Standard: implement application-layer hooks (PreToolUse with permissionDecision: 'deny') OR forced tool_choice to intercept tool calls server-side. Remove model discretion.

cards.q: What is tool context pruning and why is it needed?
cards.a: Repeated lookup_order calls fill the context with verbose shipping/payment data when only return status is needed. Application-side filtering extracts only relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, status) from each tool response before the conversation proceeds.

cards.q: What is schema redundancy and how does it enforce mathematical consistency?
cards.a: Include both calculated_total (model sums line items) and stated_total (extracted from doc). Flag for human review ONLY when they don't match. Catches OCR errors and extraction mistakes automatically, without trusting model self-assessment.

cards.q: What is the resilient catch-all pattern for enum schemas?
cards.a: Instead of expanding enums as edge cases arise (fragile), add an 'other' enum value paired with a detail string field. Captures unexpected types without validation errors while preserving structure for known categories.

cards.q: When are automated retries effective vs ineffective?
cards.a: Effective: formatting errors (nested vs flat, locale-formatted strings), resolved in 2-3 attempts with specific feedback. Ineffective: missing information (source says 'et al.' but you want the full author list), the data isn't there. Recognize when to fail fast.

cards.q: How do you resume async sessions with stale data?
cards.a: Resume with full conversation history, but programmatically filter out previous tool_result messages. Keep human/assistant turns. Forces the agent to re-fetch needed data on resumption. Returning customers always receive fresh, current information.

cards.q: What is the MCP tool specificity principle?
cards.a: Avoid broad monolithic tools (analyze_dependencies) alongside built-in tools, the agent defaults to Grep. Split into granular tools (list_imports, resolve_transitive_deps, detect_circular_deps). Enhance descriptions to detail capabilities, expected outputs, and when to prefer them.

cards.q: What is directed codebase exploration vs the anti-pattern?
cards.a: Anti-pattern: Read-load thousands of lines sequentially (context-heavy, inefficient). Pattern: start broad then pinpoint, analyze imports/base interfaces first, trace specific implementations, then dynamically generate subtasks based on findings. Use Glob/Grep before Read.

cards.q: How does Anthropic's multi-agent research system manage state between subagents?
cards.a: Subagents do NOT share memory or read each other's state. They communicate ONLY through the lead/coordinator: subagents return findings to the lead, lead synthesizes. The LEAD persists its plan to memory/filesystem to survive long contexts (>200k tokens); subagents output to filesystem and pass lightweight references back. Anti-pattern: daisy-chaining full conversation logs (exponential token cost). Note: "shared vector store between subagents with semantic search" is third-party framing, NOT Anthropic-canonical.

cards.q: How do you force execution order in a multi-step pipeline?
cards.a: tool_choice: {type: 'tool', name: 'extract_metadata'} on the first API call guarantees extraction before enrichment. Don't rely on prompt begging. Caveat: incompatible with adaptive/extended thinking, only auto/none allowed there.

cards.q: What is goal-oriented delegation vs procedural micromanagement?
cards.a: Procedural: step-by-step instructions (Search X, Read Y, Extract Z), rigid, misses emerging topics. Goal-oriented: specify research goals + quality criteria, let the subagent determine strategy. Add enum parameters (analysis_type: extraction | summarization) to guide behavior.

cards.q: What is the structured intermediate representation pattern?
cards.a: When passing findings from different agents (financial=JSON, news=prose, patent=lists) to synthesis, use a format conversion layer to standardize to a common representation (claim, evidence, source, confidence). Require all subagents to output structured claim-source mappings.

cards.q: What's the Memory + Compaction pattern for long-running agents (2026)?
cards.a: Enable server-side compaction beta (compact-2026-01-12) to summarize prior turns + memory tool (memory_20250818) to write durable facts to /memories/. Compaction handles 'what happened so far'; memory handles 'what I need to remember next time.' Together they unblock 30+ minute sessions.

cards.q: How do you protect against prompt injection via untrusted content in MCP servers?
cards.a: Vet third-party MCP servers, they're a known prompt-injection vector. Use allowedTools wildcards conservatively. Treat MCP tool annotations as UNTRUSTED. Sampling requires explicit user approval (per MCP spec). For production: prefer registered/community servers over scraped ones.

cards.q: What does the Architect's Reference Matrix show?
cards.a: For each challenge × use case: Token Bloat → strict schemas, filter stale results, scratchpad/memory, shared vector store. Latency → batch routing, prompt caching, JIT retrieval, parallelization. Compliance → schema redundancy, hooks, dontAsk, tool_choice. Accuracy → Structured Outputs, few-shot, granular MCP, structured intermediates.

cards.q: What is the Production Architecture Blueprint's three-layer structure?
cards.a: Layer 1 (Ingestion & Routing): pattern router splits real-time vs batch. Layer 2 (Multi-Agent Orchestration & Generation): execution layer with granular tools + application intercepts (validation, policy, schema checks). Layer 3 (Validation, Aggregation & Delivery): synthesis, formatting, delivery. State layer handles pruning + shared memory.

crash.val: Latency → parallelize + cache. Accuracy → structured + few-shot. Cost → Batch + pruning. Compliance → app-layer (NEVER prompts).
Hooks intercept tool calls server-side. Emphatic prompts still fail ~3%. Remove model discretion entirely.
Filter verbose responses (40+ fields → 4 relevant) app-side BEFORE they enter context.
Filter stale tool_result, keep human/asst turns. For dynamic envs, inform agent which files changed.
tool_choice: {type:'tool',name:...} on first call. Caveat: incompatible with thinking: only auto/none allowed there.
Specify GOALS + quality criteria, not procedures. Add enum params (analysis_type) to guide behavior.
Split monolithic tools into single-purpose alternatives. Enhance descriptions to detail capabilities & preference vs built-ins.

Per Anthropic's multi-agent research blog: subagents do NOT share memory; they return findings to the lead. LEAD saves its plan to memory/filesystem to survive long context; subagents output to filesystem and pass lightweight references back. Avoid daisy-chaining full logs (exponential cost). Note: "shared vector store between subagents" is third-party framing, NOT Anthropic-canonical.
Server compaction summarizes prior turns + memory tool persists durable facts. Together unblock 30+ min sessions.
calculated_total + stated_total. Flag mismatch for human review. Catches OCR / extraction errors.
Enum + 'other' + detail string. Avoid fragile enum expansion.
Standardize multi-format subagent outputs to common JSON (claim/evidence/source/confidence) via format conversion layer.
Full conversation logs between subagents = exponential token cost. Use shared vector store/files.
Step-by-step instructions to subagent ("Search X, Read Y"): rigid, misses emerging topics. Specify goals.
Sequential Read of thousands of lines is context-heavy. Pattern: Glob/Grep first → pinpoint → Read targeted.
